fact: September 29 is exactly 272 days into the year, which means there are only 93 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for squirrels to go absolutely nuts collecting acorns - they can bury up to 10,000 nuts in a single season!
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets almost exactly due west around this date because we just passed the autumn equinox a week ago.
September 29 falls smack in the middle of Libra season, making it the most balanced day for the most balanced zodiac sign.
Maple trees in cold regions are showing off their brightest reds and oranges right now because cool nights trigger them to stop making green chlorophyll.
Why do spiders seem to be everywhere in late September? Because this is mating season for many species, and the males are desperately searching for girlfriends!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, September 29 marks the beginning of serious spring fever - flowers are blooming and baby animals are being born everywhere.
The moon phase around this date changes every year, but when there's a full moon on September 29, it's called a Harvest Moon and appears extra huge and orange.
Monarch butterflies are in the middle of their epic 3,000-mile journey to Mexico right now, and some of the ones flying today have never made this trip before but somehow know exactly where to go.
In northern forests, this is peak mushroom season because the soil is still warm from summer but there's more moisture from fall rains.
Geese flying south in their famous V-formation are at their noisiest around this time because they're basically having loud family arguments about which route to take.
